lines: 4 ;This source code is for the microcontroller in the radio.  It is a
5 ;QFP-80 package and has the Delco markings "N60 FLASH" and "09370082"
6 ;on the outside.  It was decapsulated and the die markings identified
7 ;it as an NEC uPD78F0831Y.  There is no documentation for this chip
8 ;but it is very similar to the uPD78F0833Y, which is documented.
10 ;KWP1281 was used to dump the internal flash ROM contents from a radio
11 ;that had a sticker "SOFTWARE 23" on its case.  The uPD78F0831Y was
12 ;then erased and flashed with the dump.  The radio was fully functional.
14 ;No rights are claimed on the executable object code as found
15 ;in the internal flash ROM of the "N60 FLASH" chip.  The SHA-1

175 ; REGION SETTING - Controls FM/AM Frequency Ranges
177 ; The region byte determines which frequency ranges the radio can tune.
178 ; This is the primary setting users want to modify to access different bands.
193 ;     Notes: FCC compliant, default region, special tuner config
198 ;     Notes: Narrow FM band, ARIB standard
203 ;     Notes: ITU Region 1, adds 0.3 MHz to FM upper limit vs Region 0
208 ;     Notes: Extended AM range vs Region 2
228 ;     Notes: UNUSUAL 200 kHz FM steps instead of 100 kHz!
229 ;            Widest FM range but half the channel density
234 ;     - Different tuner I2C configuration (mem_bcd3 vs mem_bcde)
235 ;     - FCC-specific RF emission settings for regulatory compliance
236 ;     - Special handling at 87.8 MHz (possible NOAA weather band avoidance)
247 ;   │   - No risk of manual calculation errors                     │
255 ;   │      This is "DELCO" backwards: D=04, E=01, C=03, L=04, O=skip│
270 ;   │   7. Verify frequency range matches new region               │
283 ;   │   2. Read EEPROM using programmer → save as backup           │
288 ;   │      b. Verifies current checksum (79 bytes at 0x10-0x60)    │
289 ;   │      c. Modifies byte at 0x0046 to new region                │
298 ;   │   5. Flash modified file back to EEPROM                      │
303 ;   │ Risk: Manual checksum errors will brick radio                │
304 ;   │       Always keep backup of original EEPROM!                 │
314 ;                BUT: Uses 200 kHz FM steps (half the channels!)
327 ;   - FM band should match region (e.g., 87.5-108.0 for Region 2)
328 ;   - AM band should match region (e.g., 531-1629 for Region 2)
329 ;   - AM step should match (9 kHz or 10 kHz)
330 ;   - Radio should power on normally (no "SAFE" display)
331 ;   - All features should work (presets, scan, seek)
334 ;   Python: vw_region_change.py - Checksum calculator and EEPROM modifier
335 ;   KWP1281: Various tools support address 0x7C (VCDS, VAG-COM, etc.)
339 ;   ⚠ Wrong checksum will prevent radio from starting
340 ;   ⚠ Region changes may violate FCC/regulatory rules in your country
341 ;   ⚠ Some features may not work in non-native regions (RDS, traffic)
342 ;   ⚠ Region 7's 200 kHz FM steps may cause poor reception

499 ; MODE CONTROL VARIABLES - Primary mode state storage
501 ; These variables control which mode the radio operates in (FM, AM, CD, Tape)
502 ; and are critical to understanding mode switching behavior.
506 ;   0x01 = FM Radio (FM1 or FM2 band selected by mem_f256_ee_00b3)
508 ;   0x03 = CD Mode (requires CDC - CD Changer to be present)
509 ;   0x04 = Tape Mode (requires tape deck to be present)
513 ;   2. Key code stored in mem_fe43_key (e.g., 0x17 for FM)
514 ;   3. Mode handler validates and sets mem_f190 to target mode
515 ;   4. mem_fe7d.2 flag set to signal mode change pending
516 ;   5. Main loop compares mem_f190 with mem_fe30 at lab_42f8 (0x42F8)
517 ;   6. If different, mode transition sequence executes
518 ;   7. mem_fe30 updated to new mode, then mem_f253_ee_00b0_mode saved
521 ;   mem_f253_ee_00b0_mode (0xF253): Current mode, persisted to EEPROM
522 ;   mem_fe30 (0xFE30): Active working mode in RAM
524 ;   mem_fe7d.2: Mode change pending flag (triggers transition)
537 ;FM Sub-mode selector - Allows two independent FM bands
539 ;  0x01 = FM1 band (first set of 6 presets + frequency)
540 ;  0x02 = FM2 band (second set of 6 presets + frequency)
541 ;  Toggled by pressing FM mode key (0x17) while already in FM mode
542 ;  Each band maintains independent preset stations and current frequency

809 ; These variables track which keys are pressed and queue them for processing.
812 ;   1. sub_56cc_upd_keys (0x56CC) reads uPD16432B via SPI every ~1ms
813 ;   2. 4-byte scan code compared against key_matrix table (0xB330)
815 ;   4. Key code stored in mem_fc27 with bit 7 set (0x80 = pressed flag)
816 ;   5. Key debounced and queued in mem_fc24 (4-byte circular queue)
819 ;   8. Mode-specific handlers check mem_fe43_key to determine action
839 ;Key queue buffer - Stores up to 4 debounced key codes awaiting processing
840 ;  Keys are added by key event handler after debouncing
841 ;  Keys are extracted by sub_911b for mode handler processing
842 ;  Allows multiple rapid key presses to be buffered and processed in order
847 ;Raw key code from uPD16432B or MFSW (Multi-Function Steering Wheel)
848 ;  Bit 7: Key state flag (1 = pressed, 0 = released)
849 ;  Bits 6-0: Key code from key_matrix table lookup
850 ;  Updated by sub_56cc_upd_keys (0x56CC) after key scan and table lookup
851 ;  Examples: 0x97 = FM key pressed, 0x17 = FM key released
856 ;  Used to track position in queue for reading/writing
857 ;  Value of 0xFF indicates queue is empty

2251 ;  HL = pointer to buffer to transfer

2255 ;Flag bit mem_fe5f1.1 sets the data direction:
2256 ;  0 = Transmit and receive.  Buffer at HL will be transmitted and
2258 ;  1 = Transmit only.  Buffer at HL will be transmitted and preserved.
2265 ;Code running in the main loop initiates the transfer by setting
2266 ;up the values in RB2, initializing the two flag bits above, enabling

3150 ; Calculate 16-bit checksum of RAM area mirroring EEPROM data.
3151 ; This is the core function that protects EEPROM integrity.
3154 ;   This implements a simple 16-bit sum with initial value 0x55:
3165 ;     - X register holds low byte of sum (initialized to 0x55)
3166 ;     - A register holds high byte of sum (initialized to 0x00)
3167 ;     - Loop adds each data byte to X, propagating carry to A
3171 ;   Checksums stored in little-endian (Intel byte order):
3177 ;           Contains: Region, SAFE code, Soft Coding
3190 ;      - Write new checksum to storage address
3197 ;     movw hl,#mem_f1b3_ee_0010+1      ; HL = start addr (note: function decrements)
3200 ;     callf !eeram_csum_no_update      ; Sets X=0x55, Carry=1, calls eeram_csum
3201 ;     bz checksum_ok                   ; Branch if Z set (checksum matches)
3204 ;   Example 2: Calculate and Update Checksum Area 1
3208 ;     callf !eeram_csum_update         ; Sets X=0x55, Carry=0, calls eeram_csum
3209 ;     ; Checksum now calculated and stored at [DE] and [DE+1]

3215 ;     ; This is why KWP1281 method doesn't require manual checksum!
3219 ;     HL = Starting RAM address that mirrors EEPROM data

3222 ;     A  = Number of bytes to checksum
3234 ;          1 = Calculated matches stored (or was successfully stored)

3257 ;     - B counts down from num_bytes to 1
3258 ;     - HL is decremented before loop starts
3259 ;     - So actual address accessed is: (HL_original - 1) + B
3260 ;     - For B = num_bytes: accesses HL_original + num_bytes - 1 (last byte)
3262 ;   This allows counting down (more efficient) while accessing forward
3267 ;   - Detects accidental corruption, not malicious modification

5265 ; PRESET BUTTON HANDLER - SHORT PRESS vs LONG PRESS DETECTION
5267 ; Handles preset buttons 1-6 for FM1, FM2, and AM bands
5270 ;   SHORT PRESS (quick tap): Recalls station from preset
5271 ;   LONG PRESS  (hold ~1s):  Stores current station to preset
5274 ;   The handler runs MULTIPLE TIMES per key press in a loop/state machine.
5275 ;   Each iteration checks if the key is STILL being physically held.
5277 ;   1st iteration: Key still held → mem_f1ab = 0x80 (short press assumed)
5278 ;   2nd+ iterations: Key still held → mem_f1ab stays 0x80
5279 ;   Final iteration: Key released → Check previous state:
5280 ;       - If mem_f1ab was 0x80 (was held): Change to 0xC0 = LONG PRESS
5281 ;       - If this is first check: Set 0xC0 = LONG PRESS
5283 ;   So: LONG PRESS = Key was held across multiple iterations then released
5290 ;   Format: Frequency INDEX (not MHz/kHz), converted using region tables
5294 ;   mem_fb5b: Last pressed preset for FM1 (0x01-0x06)
5295 ;   mem_fb5c: Last pressed preset for FM2 (0x01-0x06)
16B1 BC 5C 41      [ 9] 5303     bt mem_fe5c.3,lab_16f5  ;16b1  bc 5c 41     Branch if preset processing flag 3 set
16B7 9A 0B 08      [ 7] 5305     call !sub_080b_fm1fm2   ;16b7  9a 0b 08     Get FM1/FM2 band: A=0 (FM1) or 1 (FM2)
16BA 73            [ 2] 5306     mov b,a                 ;16ba  73           B = FM band selector (0 or 1)
16BB 16 5B FB      [ 6] 5307     movw hl,#mem_fb5b       ;16bb  16 5b fb     HL = preset storage base address
16BE F0 43         [ 5] 5308     mov a,mem_fe43_key      ;16be  f0 43        A = processed key code (0x01-0x06 for presets)
16C0 1D 00         [ 4] 5309     sub a,#0x00             ;16c0  1d 00        (no-op subtraction, just clears flags)
16C2 9E 5A FB      [ 9] 5310     mov !mem_fb5a,a         ;16c2  9e 5a fb     Store preset number globally (mem_fb5a)
16C5 BB            [ 7] 5311     mov [hl+b],a            ;16c5  bb           Store to mem_fb5b (FM1) or mem_fb5c (FM2)

13740 ; MODE KEY DETECTION AND HANDLER DISPATCH
13742 ; This section detects when a mode key (FM/AM/CD/TAPE) is pressed and
13743 ; dispatches to the appropriate mode handler routine.
13745 ; Called from: Main task processing loop during regular operation
13746 ; Frequency: Checked every task cycle when conditions are met
13749 ;   1. Check if mode changes are currently allowed (state flags)
13750 ;   2. Get current key code from mem_fe43_key (set by sub_911b)
13752 ;      0x17 = FM mode key (button labeled "FM" on faceplate)
13753 ;      0x16 = AM mode key (button labeled "AM" on faceplate)
13754 ;      0x14 = CD mode key (button labeled "CD" on faceplate)
13755 ;      0x15 = Tape mode key (button labeled "TAPE" on faceplate)
13758 ;      a. Set A = 0xFF to signal mode change request
13759 ;      b. Look up handler address in mem_b170 table (line 33559)
13761 ;      d. Handler validates request (checks hardware availability, etc.)
13762 ;      e. Handler sets mem_f190 to target mode value if valid
13763 ;      f. Handler sets mem_fe7d.2 flag if mode change approved
13764 ;      g. Handler returns to continue processing
13765 ;   5. Compare mem_f190 with mem_fe30 at lab_42f8 (0x42F8)
13766 ;   6. If different, set mem_fe7d.2 flag to trigger transition
13767 ;   7. Mode transition happens in subsequent main loop cycles
13770 ;   1. Choose unused mode value (e.g., 0x05 for AUX mode)
13771 ;   2. Write handler routine following existing pattern (validate, set mem_f190)
13772 ;   3. Add handler address to mem_b170 table (0xB170)
13773 ;   4. Add key code comparison here at lab_427a (0x427A)
13774 ;   5. Map button to new key code in key_matrix table (0xB330)
13775 ;   6. Implement mode-specific initialization and processing code
4285 AD 1A         [ 6]13784     bz lab_42a1             ;4285  ad 1a        Yes->signal mode change
428A AD 15         [ 6]13788     bz lab_42a1             ;428a  ad 15        Yes->signal mode change
428C C8 43 48      [ 8]13791     cmp mem_fe43_key,#0x48  ;428c  c8 43 48     Key = unknown mode?
428F AD 10         [ 6]13792     bz lab_42a1             ;428f  ad 10        Yes->signal mode change
4294 AD 0B         [ 6]13797     bz lab_42a1             ;4294  ad 0b        Yes->signal mode change
4299 AD 06         [ 6]13801     bz lab_42a1             ;4299  ad 06        Yes->signal mode change
42A1 A1 FF         [ 4]13807     mov a,#0xff             ;42a1  a1 ff

13858 ; This comparison is the KEY mechanism that triggers actual mode changes!
13860 ; If mem_f190 (target mode set by handler) differs from mem_fe30 (current

14707 ;Perform an I2C transaction.  The request block contains a control byte
14718 ;    xx  I2C Data byte 0 (I2C Address) / I2C Data byte 5  kwp_rx_buf+6 -> kwp_cu_buf+3 -> kwp_cu_i2c_buf
14719 ;    xx  I2C Data byte 1               / I2C Data byte 6  kwp_rx_buf+7 -> kwp_cu_buf+4 -> kwp_cu_i2c_buf+1
14720 ;    xx  I2C Data byte 2               / I2C Data byte 7  kwp_rx_buf+8 -> kwp_cu_buf+5 -> kwp_cu_i2c_buf+2
14721 ;    xx  I2C Data byte 3               / I2C Data byte 8  kwp_rx_buf+9 -> kwp_cu_buf+6 -> kwp_cu_i2c_buf+3
14722 ;    xx  I2C Data byte 4               / I2C Data byte 9  kwp_rx_buf+a -> kwp_cu_buf+7 -> kwp_cu_i2c_buf+4
14727 ;  Bit 7 = Flag: 0=do actual I2C, 1=write to I2C buffer kwp_cu_i2c_buf only
14729 ;  Bit 5 | Number of bytes to write to I2C (0-10)
14730 ;  Bit 4 | (first 5 bytes from kwp_rx_buf+6, second 5 bytes from kwp_cu_i2c_buf)
14733 ;  Bit 1 | Number of bytes to read from I2C (0-5)
14738 ;  - Perform an I2C write only       (number of bytes to write > 0, number to read = 0)
14739 ;  - Perform an I2C read only        (number of bytes to write = 0, number to read > 0)
14740 ;  - Perform an I2C write then read  (both lengths non-zero)
14741 ;  - Do not perform I2C; write to kwp_cu_i2c_buf used for I2C writes > 5 bytes
14743 ;If Bit 7 of the control byte is set, no I2C operation will be performed
14744 ;and the other bits of the control byte are ignored.  The 5 data bytes in
14745 ;the request are written to the buffer kwp_cu_i2c_buf.  When a future
14746 ;request block specifies an I2C write longer than 5 bytes, the first 5
14747 ;bytes will come from that request block and up to five more will come
14758 ;    xx  I2C Data byte 0 (I2C Address) / I2C Data byte 5  kwp_tx_buf+6 <- kwp_cu_buf+3
14759 ;    xx  I2C Data byte 1               / I2C Data byte 6  kwp_tx_buf+7 <- kwp_cu_buf+4
14760 ;    xx  I2C Data byte 2               / I2C Data byte 7  kwp_tx_buf+8 <- kwp_cu_buf+5
14761 ;    xx  I2C Data byte 3               / I2C Data byte 8  kwp_tx_buf+9 <- kwp_cu_buf+6
14762 ;    xx  I2C Data byte 4               / I2C Data byte 9  kwp_tx_buf+a <- kwp_cu_buf+7
14765 ;The control byte and 5 data bytes will always be returned.  The data bytes
14766 ;will contain the data written or read.  If fewer than 5 bytes were read or
14767 ;written, the extra bytes in the response block will contain whatever was
14768 ;in those positions in the request block. (TODO verify this)
14770 ;If the control byte = 0, it means an an I2C operation was requested (bit 7 clear)
14771 ;with no bytes to read or write.  The data returned in the response block is

16597 ; KWP1281 WRITE EEPROM COMMAND HANDLER (Address 0x7C, Title 0x2B)
16599 ; Handles KWP1281 "Write EEPROM" command on DELCO compatibility address (0x7C)
16600 ; This is the SAFE and RECOMMENDED method for changing region and other settings
16602 ; WHY USE KWP1281 INSTEAD OF DIRECT EEPROM MODIFICATION:
16603 ;   ✓ Firmware automatically recalculates checksums (no manual math!)
16605 ;   ✓ Protects critical addresses (SAFE code, soft coding)
16606 ;   ✓ No EEPROM removal required (works via K-line cable)
16607 ;   ✓ Safer than manual modification (firmware controls everything)
16608 ;   ✗ Requires KWP1281 tool (VAG-COM, VCDS, or generic adapter)
16611 ;   Must login on address 0x7C with code: 04148 decimal (0x1034 hex)
16621 ;   This is a backward compatibility feature for DELCO radios
16622 ;   All VW Premium radios support this for dealer service tools
16624 ; PROTECTED ADDRESSES (writes silently ignored with fake success):
16628 ;     - Write attempts return success but don't actually write
16634 ;     - Write attempts return success but don't actually write
16639 ;   - Invalid region values don't brick radio
16650 ;   4             | AddrL | 0x46  | EEPROM address low byte (e.g., 0x46 for region)
16651 ;   5             | Num   | 0x01  | Number of bytes to write
16652 ;   6             | Data  | value | Data byte(s) to write
16653 ;   n             | End   | 0x03  | End of block marker
16671 ;      c. Send complement of 3rd keyword byte
16692 ;      b. Checks if address is protected (0x0014-0x0015, 0x0058-0x0060)
16693 ;      c. Writes byte to RAM mirror at 0xF1E7
16696 ;      f. Later commits RAM mirror to physical EEPROM via I2C
16702 ;      - Wait 10+ seconds for capacitors to discharge
16704 ;      - Radio boots with new region settings
16707 ;      - Check FM range matches new region
16708 ;      - Check AM range matches new region
16712 ;   When firmware writes to EEPROM addresses in checksum areas:
16713 ;   1. Byte is written to RAM mirror (e.g., 0xF1E7 for region)
16714 ;   2. Running checksum accumulator is updated with new byte value
16715 ;   3. When write operation completes, final checksum is calculated
16716 ;   4. Checksum stored at appropriate location (0xF202-0xF203 for Area 1)
16717 ;   5. Later, RAM mirror committed to physical EEPROM with valid checksum
16719 ;   This is why KWP1281 is recommended:
16721 ;   - NO risk of checksum errors bricking radio
16729 ;     ├─→ sub_2c33: Parse and validate write request
16731 ;     │     ├─→ Read address and length from KWP buffer
16739 ;     │           ├─→ Write to RAM mirror (e.g., 0xF1E7 for region)
16743 ;     │                 ├─→ Add new byte to running checksum
16747 ;     ├─→ Send ACK response to scan tool
16749 ;     └─→ Later: Commit RAM mirror to physical EEPROM
16755 ;   - Generic KWP1281 tools (Python scripts, Arduino sketches)
16756 ;   - Some OBD-II adapters with custom software
16767 ;   sub_2c33 (0x2C33): Parses KWP request, performs EEPROM write
16768 ;   eeprom_write (0x628E): Low-level I2C write to physical EEPROM
16769 ;   eeram_f206_wr_byte_hl (0x4092): Writes to RAM with checksum update

17271 ;kwp_con_3f_state is the state of the connection to the cluster (address 0x3F):
17272 ;  kwp_3f_09_ack expects 0x01, then sets it to 0x02, then sends security access request (title 0xd7)
17273 ;  kwp_3f_06_disconnect unconditionally sets it to 0x00, then ends session
17274 ;  kwp_3f_0a_nak expects 0x01, then sets it to 0x00
17275 ;  kwp_3f_3d_secure_access expects 0x02, then sets it to 0x00, then ends session
17277 ;This flow is just like the Premium 4 firmware at mem_e3bd.
17280 ;  Radio sends 5 baud init to instrument cluster on address 0x3F.
17281 ;    The cluster must use 10400 baud because the radio hardcodes the baud rate.
17282 ;  Cluster sends a 0x09 ACK block after the send keyword byte exchange.
17283 ;  Radio sends a 0xD7 Security Access Request block (lab_55de_secure_req).
17284 ;  Cluster sends a 0x3D Security Access Response block (sub_2537_descramble_and_lock_or_unlock).
17285 ;  Radio sends a 0x06 Disconnect block (no response from cluster; end of communication).

17881 ;Send NAK response asking for the last block to be sent again
17883 ;To ask the sender to resend the last block, the "Block counter to resend"
17884 ;will be set to the block counter of the last block received.
17886 ;This type of NAK is returned under only two conditions:
17888 ;  1. A bad block end byte was received (probably due to line noise).  The block
17889 ;     end byte is special because it is the only one where we do not send back
17890 ;     a complement.  Instead, we ask the block to be sent again if it is bad.
17892 ;  2. The block title is a known one, but the block length is not one that is
17893 ;     expected for that title.  It's not clear why we ask the block to be sent again
17894 ;     for this case because it should not help.  When the tester sent the block
17895 ;     length, we sent back the complement, so the sender should have detected if
17896 ;     the length was corrupted on the line and dealt with it at that moment.
17902 ;   xx  Block counter to resend   kwp_tx_buf+3  (Counter of the request block)

17908     ;Block counter to resend = Block counter of the request block

534C 51            [ 2]17910     dec a                   ;534c  51           Subtract 1 to get counter of last block received
534D BB            [ 7]17911     mov [hl+b],a            ;534d  bb           Store in KWP1281 tx buffer byte 3 (Block counter to resend)
5351 BB            [ 7]17915     mov [hl+b],a            ;5351  bb           Store in KWP1281 tx buffer byte 4
5352 9B F7 34      [ 6]17917     br !send_kwp_tx_buf     ;5352  9b f7 34     Set flags to start sending the KWP1281 tx buffer

18732 ; Primary function that reads key presses from uPD16432B display controller
18735 ; Called from: Task 0x06 main loop, approximately every 1ms
18738 ;   1. Check preconditions (state flags must allow key reading)
18739 ;   2. Configure SPI hardware for uPD16432B communication
18740 ;   3. Send "Read Key Data" command (0x4C) to uPD16432B
18741 ;   4. Receive 4 bytes of key scan data via interrupt-driven SPI
18742 ;   5. Mask unused bits using key_mask table (0xB3A7)
18743 ;   6. Compare with previous scan data to detect changes
18745 ;      a. Search key_matrix table (0xB330) for matching scan pattern
18746 ;      b. Extract key code from first byte of matching entry
18747 ;      c. Set bit 7 if key is currently pressed (0x80 | key_code)
18749 ;      e. Call sub_0994 to process key event (debouncing, queuing)
18750 ;   8. Update previous scan data for next comparison
18752 ; Hardware interface - SPI to uPD16432B display controller:
18753 ;   P47 (Pin 28): STB - Chip select for uPD16432B (active high)
18755 ;   P31 (Pin 30): SO30 - SPI data out (MCU to uPD16432B)
18756 ;   P30 (Pin 29): SI30 - SPI data in (uPD16432B to MCU)
18759 ;   Returns 4 bytes where each bit represents one button position in matrix
18760 ;   Only one bit should be set at a time (single key press detection)
18761 ;   Byte 0: Masked to 0x00 (not used by this faceplate design)
18772 ; Returns: Nothing (updates mem_fc27 global and triggers key event processing)
18776 ;Read uPD16432B key data if ??? conditions are met

19192 ; External interrupt triggered by POWER key on pin P04 (Pin 49 of MCU).
19196 ;   Pin: P04 (physical pin 49 on QFP-80 package)
19199 ;   Interrupt: Configured for both edges (rising and falling)
19200 ;   Priority: Can be configured via interrupt priority registers
19202 ; Behavior depends on current power state:
19206 ;       - Set mem_fe67.0 flag to trigger power-on sequence
19209 ;       - Power-on sequence will start in main loop
19212 ;       - Set mem_fe7d.6 flag for special POWER key handling
19213 ;       - May trigger menu or special function
19221 ;   1. Turn on switched 5V supply via P80 pin
19223 ;      - uPD16432B display controller (send init commands via SPI)
19224 ;      - TEA6840H tuner IC (send config via I2C)
19233 ;   5. Update display with mode and frequency
19234 ;   6. Send initial FIS message if instrument cluster connected
19239 ;   - This runs in interrupt context - keep processing minimal
19240 ;   - Main work done by setting flags for main loop to process
19245 ;Falling edge: Sets mem_fe67.0 (radio off) or mem_fe7d.6 (radio on)
19248 ;Fires on the rising or falling edge of the POWER key
590D CC 00 03      [ 9]19255     bt p0.4,lab_5913        ;590d  cc 00 03       Branch if the POWER key is not being pressed

19295 ; External interrupt triggered by STOP/EJECT key on pin P06 (Pin 51 of MCU).
19299 ;   Pin: P06 (physical pin 51 on QFP-80 package)
19302 ;   Interrupt: Configured for falling edge only (key press)
19303 ;   Priority: Can be configured via interrupt priority registers
19306 ;   Simply sets mem_fe7d.7 flag when key is pressed (if allowed by mem_fe62.1)
19307 ;   Actual functionality is implemented in mode-specific code that polls this flag
19309 ; Mode-specific behavior (implemented in main loop code, not in interrupt):
19312 ;     - Send CDC eject command via serial protocol
19315 ;     - May auto-switch to radio mode after eject
19325 ;     - Or could implement scan stop function
19332 ;   - This interrupt only sets a flag - it doesn't execute the action
19333 ;   - The actual eject/stop functionality is in mode handler code
19334 ;   - Mode handlers check mem_fe7d.7 and respond accordingly
19335 ;   - Flag must be cleared by the mode handler after processing
19336 ;   - If mem_fe62.1 is set, key press is ignored (safety interlock)
19339 ;   - Hardware or software debouncing may be used
19340 ;   - Mode handlers may add additional delay before acting
19343 ;   - mem_fe62.1 flag prevents action during certain operations
19344 ;   - Protects against accidental ejects during critical operations

23393 ; AM (MEDIUM WAVE) FREQUENCY CONVERSION BY REGION
23395 ; Converts frequency index to actual AM frequency in kHz based on regional
23396 ; broadcast allocations following ITU (International Telecommunication Union)
23402 ;   Base: 530 kHz,  Step: 10 kHz,  Range: 530-1720 kHz*  (*XXX Bug: should be 1710)
23405 ;   Standard: FCC Part 73, Extended AM band (ECAM) to 1705 kHz
23406 ;   Max Index: 0x77 (119) = 1720 kHz XXX Bug: EXCEEDS SPEC (should be 0x76 = 1710)
23409 ;   Base: 522 kHz,  Step: 9 kHz,   Range: 522-1638 kHz
23414 ;   Note: 522 kHz start is UNIQUE to Japan (aligns with 9 kHz grid)
23417 ;   Base: 531 kHz,  Step: 9 kHz,   Range: 531-1638 kHz
23420 ;   Standard: Geneva Frequency Plan (1975), extended beyond 1602 kHz
23422 ;   Markets: Germany, France, UK, Netherlands, Belgium, Spain, Italy
23425 ;   Base: 531 kHz,  Step: 9 kHz,   Range: 531-1629 kHz
23429 ;   Note: EXACTLY 9 kHz (ONE STATION) less than Region 2!
23430 ;   Purpose: Possibly Poland, Czech Rep, Hungary, former East Germany
23431 ;   Reason: Historical allocation? Cross-border interference mitigation?
23434 ;   IDENTICAL to Region 0 (530-1720* kHz, 10 kHz steps)
23435 ;   Purpose: Possibly different vehicle platforms, model years, or markets
23436 ;   All point to same frequency table to save ROM space
23439 ;   Base: 520 kHz,  Step: 10 kHz,   Range: 520-1710 kHz
23443 ;   Note: LOWEST base frequency (10 kHz below standard MW band)
23444 ;   Theory: Brazil allows experimental/community stations below 530 kHz
23447 ;   9 kHz:  ITU Region 1 (Europe, Africa, Middle East, Asia)
23448 ;           - High population density = more channels needed
23459 ;   - 9 kHz region in 10 kHz area: Misses 50% of stations!
23460 ;     Example: Europe setting in USA can tune 540, 549, 558...
23461 ;              but NOT 550, 560, 570... (all the even x10 stations)
23463 ;   - 10 kHz region in 9 kHz area: Off-frequency for most stations
23464 ;     Example: USA setting in Europe tunes 540, 550, 560...
23467 ;   Only overlap: Every 90 kHz (LCM of 9 and 10)
23471 ;   Function freq_idx_constrain (0x1AD1) ensures index stays within
23472 ;   region's maximum. This prevents the Preset 5 bug (0x77 = 1720 kHz)
23473 ;   from causing crashes - it gets clamped to valid maximum at runtime.
23478 ;   Differences likely in AGC timing and IF bandwidth for 9/10 kHz spacing
6F06 12 0A 02      [ 6]23483     movw bc,#0x020a                 ;6f06  12 0a 02     BC = 522 (base = 522 kHz for Japan)

34698 ; Dispatches to mode-specific handler routines when mode keys are pressed.
34699 ; Referenced at lab_42ad (0x42AD) during mode key processing.
34702 ;   First byte: Number of entries (0x0F = 15 handlers)
34706 ;   Input: B register = handler index (determined by mode detection logic)
34707 ;   table_get_word function loads handler address into DE register
34708 ;   Code jumps to handler via "br ax" instruction
34711 ;   1. Validate mode change is currently allowed
34712 ;      - Check various state flags (mem_fe2c, mem_fe2d, etc.)
34713 ;      - Verify system is in correct state for mode change
34714 ;   2. Verify required hardware is present and functional
34715 ;      - CDC (CD Changer) must be present for CD mode
34716 ;      - Tape deck must be present for Tape mode
34718 ;   3. If valid, set mem_f190 to target mode value
34723 ;   4. Set mem_fe7d.2 flag to signal mode change approved
34724 ;   5. Return to caller (mode comparison code at lab_42f8)
34727 ;   - Handler index is NOT the same as mode value in mem_f253_ee_00b0_mode!
34728 ;   - The relationship is complex and mediated through other lookup tables
34729 ;   - Some handlers may handle multiple related functions
34730 ;   - Handlers may reject mode changes by not setting mem_f190/mem_fe7d.2
34733 ;   1. Write handler routine following existing pattern:
34739 ;   2. Add handler address to this table (increase count byte too)
34740 ;   3. Update mode detection code at lab_427a to call new handler
34741 ;   4. Add mode initialization code that executes when mem_fe7d.2 processed
34742 ;   5. Add mode-specific processing in main loop
34745 ;MODE HANDLER TABLE - Jump table for mode-specific handlers
34746 ;Used at lab_42ad (0x42AD). Handler validates and sets mem_f190, mem_fe7d.2

B17B D3 45             34756     .word lab_45d3          ;Handler 0x05: FM Mode Entry (alternate path)

34767 ;Table of addresses that are exposed for arbitrary memory read/write
34768 ;over KWP1281 on address 0x7C using these commands:
34773 ;Each command accepts an index to an address in this table and a signed
34775 ;The following contiguous regions can therefore be accessed:
34783 ;The stack grows from FE1F (stack_top) down to FCA5 (stack_bottom).
34784 ;Large portions of the stack are writable, which might lead to
34787 ;Possible locations where shellcode could be uploaded:
34789 ;  F08A-F189 is the KWP1281 receive buffer (256 bytes) but since
34790 ;            KWP1281 request blocks are normally less than ~16 bytes,
34791 ;            only that many bytes in the buffer are used.  The rest
34792 ;            will not be disturbed unless a very long KWP1281 request
34793 ;            block is sent.  F133-F189 of it is writable (87 bytes).
34795 ;  F26E-F7FF is unused; F26E-F2D6 of it is writable (105 bytes).
34797 ;  FE82-FECA is unused, all of which is writable (73 bytes).

35190 ; Translates raw 4-byte scan codes from uPD16432B display controller into
35191 ; logical key codes used by the firmware.
35193 ; Table format: Each entry is 5 bytes
35194 ;   Byte 0: Key code (0x01-0x18) assigned to this button
35195 ;   Bytes 1-4: Scan pattern from uPD16432B (only one bit set across all 4 bytes)
35197 ; The uPD16432B returns 4 bytes where each bit represents one button position
35198 ; in the key matrix. When a button is pressed, exactly one bit is set to 1.
35204 ;   Byte 4: Not used by this faceplate (masked to 0x00)
35218 ;   1. Determine the scan pattern by monitoring uPD16432B output
35219 ;      (use oscilloscope on SPI lines or add debug code)
35220 ;   2. Choose an unused key code (0x19-0x7F available)
35221 ;   3. Add a new 5-byte entry to this table:
35223 ;   4. Update key_mask if new bits are used
35224 ;   5. Implement handler code for the new key code in mode handlers
35226 ; To remap a button to different function:
35227 ;   1. Find the entry matching your button's scan pattern
35228 ;   2. Change byte 0 (key code) to the desired function's key code
35229 ;   3. Example: Make PRESET_1 button act like SEEK_UP
35234 ; Scan code lookup algorithm (used by sub_56cc_upd_keys):
35235 ;   For each entry in table (24 entries total):
35236 ;     Compare 4 scan bytes with current scan from uPD16432B
35239 ;       key_code |= 0x80 if key is pressed (set bit 7)
35246 ;On each line, the first byte is an arbitrary number.  The four bytes
35247 ;are uPD16432B key scan codes (only 1 bit is set in all 4 bytes).
B330 01 00 00 04 00    35250     .byte 0x01,   0x00, 0x00, 0x04, 0x00    ;PRESET_1 - Short: recall preset, Long: store
35277 ;Mask bytes that are AND'ed with the key data buffer (upd_keys) to
35278 ;remove unused key bits.  Byte 0 is not used by the faceplate.
35282 ;MFSW key codes supported by the radio
35293 ;indexed by a value from mfsw_codes table above
35351 ; Defines frequency ranges and tuning steps for different global regions
35354 ;   First byte: Number of region entries (8 regions: 0-7)
35355 ;   Following words: Pointers to region-specific frequency validation tables
35359 ; VW's strategy: One firmware binary, multiple global markets
35360 ;   - Single part number reduces manufacturing complexity
35362 ;   - Allows same radio hardware for different vehicles/markets
35365 ; Regional Frequency Allocations and Market Identification:
35367 ; Region 0: North America - NAR (North American Region) PRIMARY
35373 ;   SPECIAL: Only region with unique tuner I2C configuration (mem_bcd3)
35374 ;   Note: 87.7 MHz start avoids TV Channel 6 (82-88 MHz) per FCC rules
35375 ;         Gets different TEA6840H tuner config optimized for FCC emissions
35383 ;   Note: UNIQUE 76-90 MHz FM band (lowest start frequency)
35384 ;         Narrow band due to Japanese spectrum allocation
35385 ;         9 kHz AM spacing per ITU Region 1
35388 ;   Markets: Western Europe (Germany, France, UK, Benelux, etc.)
35392 ;   Standard: ITU Region 1 (9 kHz AM spacing)
35393 ;   Note: Widest standard FM band (87.5-108.0 MHz)
35397 ;   Markets: Eastern Europe (Poland, Czech Republic, former East Germany?)
35399 ;   AM: 531 - 1620 kHz, 9 kHz steps (9 kHz LESS than Region 2!)
35401 ;   Standard: ITU Region 1 (9 kHz AM spacing)
35403 ;         Differs ONLY in AM upper limit (1620 vs 1629 kHz)
35404 ;         Likely legacy from different Eastern European broadcast allocations
35407 ;   Markets: Unknown - Possibly Canada, or specific US vehicle platform
35411 ;     - Canadian market (bilingual requirements, different soft coding)
35417 ;   Markets: Unknown - Possibly Mexico, or different US vehicle platform
35436 ;   Markets: SPECULATION - Brazil (strongest evidence) or custom
35437 ;   FM: 75.9 - 108.1 MHz, 200 kHz steps (DOUBLE step size!)
35440 ;   Standard: ITU Region 2 (10 kHz AM), but unusual FM configuration
35441 ;   Note: UNIQUE 200 kHz FM steps - only region with this
35443 ;         75.9 MHz start is lowest of ANY region (below Japan's 76.0)
35444 ;         THEORY: Brazil has VW manufacturing + unique radio regulations
35445 ;         200 kHz steps may accommodate educational/community radio
35446 ;         or low-density areas with less channel congestion
35450 ;   - Only region tested with (region & 0x07 == 0) - see sub_1c9d_region
35451 ;   - Gets DIFFERENT tuner I2C configuration (mem_bcd3 vs mem_bcde)
35452 ;   - Tuner optimized for strict FCC RF emission requirements
35453 ;   - Primary target market (likely USA - highest volume)
35454 ;   - Special frequency handling at 87.8 MHz (possible NOAA weather avoidance)
35457 ;   Regions 0,4,5,6: North America (10 kHz AM, 87.7-107.9 MHz FM)
35458 ;   Regions 2,3:     Europe (9 kHz AM, 87.5-108.0 MHz FM)
35459 ;   Region 1:        Japan (9 kHz AM, 76.0-90.0 MHz FM - narrow!)
35460 ;   Region 7:        Brazil? (10 kHz AM, 75.9-108.1 MHz FM - 200 kHz steps!)
35464 ;   All others: Use mem_bcde I2C data (ITU-standard)
35465 ;   Difference: TEA6840H tuner chip gets different AGC/IF/bandwidth settings
35466 ;   Purpose: Regulatory compliance for RF emissions
35469 ;   FM: Index 0x00-0x?? where frequency = base_freq + (index * step)
35470 ;       For most regions: step = 100 kHz (0.1 MHz)
35471 ;       For Region 7: step = 200 kHz (0.2 MHz) - DOUBLED!
35474 ;   AM: Index 0x00-0x?? where frequency = base_freq + (index * step)
35479 ;   The tables pointed to by mem_b3d9 (mem_b3ea, mem_b3f6, mem_b3f9, mem_b3fc)
35480 ;   contain lists of frequency indices. These are used by table_find_byte to
35481 ;   validate frequencies. The purpose of these specific indices is not fully
35488 ; Note: Region value stored in mem_f1e7_ee_0046_region (EEPROM 0046)
35489 ;       Default value in firmware is 0x00 (North America/NAR/USA)

37746 ; The radio uses a TEA6840H FM/AM tuner IC for RF reception
37749 ;   - FM Range: 76-108 MHz (designed for FM broadcast band)
37750 ;   - AM Range: 531-1602 kHz (MW), 150-285 kHz (LW) in some variants
37751 ;   - PLL Tuning: I2C controlled phase-locked loop
37754 ;   - Demodulation: FM (frequency modulation) and AM (amplitude modulation)
37757 ;   - Bit-banged I2C on P7.3 (SCL) and P7.4 (SDA)
37759 ;   - Configuration data varies by region (see mem_bcd3 vs mem_bcde)
37762 ;   - Hardware VCO designed for 76-108 MHz FM band
37763 ;   - Cannot tune to VHF air band (118-137 MHz) without hardware mods
37764 ;   - PLL divider ratios fixed for FM broadcast frequencies
37768 ;     * Optimized for FCC RF emission compliance
37769 ;     * Different AGC/IF settings than other regions
37771 ;   - All Other Regions: Use mem_bcde configuration (0xBCDE)
